Q1: I kept a very strict routine because that's the only way I could manage eight online courses at once. Every morning I would wake up early, around 5:30 AM, and study for about four hours before my other responsibilities. I had a schedule written on paper that I taped to my wall, showing which course to focus on each day. It was exhausting but necessary because with limited resources, I couldn't afford to fall behind and repeat anything.
Q2: I tried to keep regular times, but honestly deadlines would sometimes throw everything off. Most weeks I studied at the same hours because my body just got used to it, but when multiple assignments were due close together, I had to adjust and sometimes study late into the night. The routine was my anchor though—without it I would have been completely lost managing everything.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I had a specific task to complete—checking if an assignment was posted, submitting work, or seeing if grades were released. Sometimes I logged in just to make sure I hadn't missed any announcements because I was always worried about missing a deadline. The gamified tasks they introduced actually made me want to log in more because earning those small achievements felt rewarding even when I was feeling down.
Q4: Yes, logging in became automatic, like brushing my teeth. After about the third week, I would just open the LMS first thing when I sat down to study without even thinking about it. The routine developed because I forced myself to do it at the same time every day until it stuck. It wasn't exciting, but it became normal, and normal felt safe.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful because I could watch them and take notes by hand, which helps me learn better since I'm a kinesthetic learner. I also found the assignment submission pages helpful because they clearly showed what was expected. The gamified task badges actually motivated me more than I expected—seeing that progress bar fill up gave me a small sense of accomplishment during difficult days.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums because I didn't have time to engage deeply with other students' opinions when I was juggling so many courses. I would read a few posts occasionally but rarely contributed because it felt like extra work that wouldn't directly help my grades. Some supplementary readings I also skipped because I had to prioritize what was absolutely necessary.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. I would ask myself, 'Will this help me pass?' and if the answer was yes, I did it thoroughly. The gamified elements also made some tasks feel more worthwhile because there was an immediate reward, even if it was just virtual points. With limited time and energy, I had to be strategic.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required activities got my full attention because they affected my grade, but optional ones I would only do if I had extra time, which was rare. There was always a small guilt about skipping optional content, but I had to be realistic about what I could handle. The gamified optional tasks were slightly different because the reward made them feel less optional.
Q9: Absolutely, I did most of my actual studying offline. I would download lecture slides and PDFs, then study from printed copies because I learn better when I can touch and highlight physical materials. I also used my own handwritten notes extensively and sometimes borrowed textbooks from friends since I couldn't afford to buy all of them. A lot of my learning happened away from any screen.
Q10: Many times I would spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or practicing problems on paper, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I also studied with a small group of classmates in person sometimes, quizzing each other and explaining concepts, but the LMS had no idea that was happening. My best learning moments were often the quiet ones with just me and my notebook.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach. I would access a video or document once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never open it again online. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab everything I needed in one session and then work offline. The LMS would show I only accessed something once, but I might have reviewed those notes ten times.
Q12: There were definitely hard moments, especially mid-semester when everything piled up and I was feeling quite sad about personal things. Some days I would sit down to study and just feel this heaviness that made it hard to focus. The stress was high even though I kept pushing through. Those weeks I did the minimum to stay on track but couldn't engage deeply.
Q13: Fatigue affected me the most. With 26 hours of studying each week across eight courses, I was often exhausted. When I was too tired, I would log into the LMS and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities at home also meant sometimes I had to choose between studying and helping family, and family usually won even if it hurt my engagement.
Q14: Yes, sometimes I would open videos or readings and my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about money worries or feeling sad, and the words on the screen just wouldn't register. I might spend an hour 'studying' but retain almost nothing. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts the most because they felt less urgent than assignments. Even though I knew participation mattered, I kept telling myself I'd do it later, and later often never came. I think I avoided them because writing my thoughts publicly felt vulnerable, and I was already dealing with enough emotional weight. It was easier to focus on tasks I could do alone.
Q16: Deadlines controlled everything. I organized my entire week around what was due and when. The days before a deadline, my LMS activity would spike because I was checking requirements, submitting work, and making sure everything was correct. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged as consistently because they gave structure to my otherwise overwhelming workload.
Q17: Feedback motivated me to keep going, especially when it was positive. Seeing a good grade made me feel like my sacrifices were worth it. When feedback was critical, I tried to use it to improve, but sometimes it made me feel more stressed. I would check for feedback regularly because knowing where I stood helped me plan my next steps.
Q18: The course structure helped me because it was predictable. I knew when modules would be released and when things were due, so I could plan ahead. Having clear weekly expectations made it easier to build my routine around the course. If the structure had been chaotic or constantly changing, I would have struggled much more.
Q19: The clear deadlines and organized modules made engagement easier. What made it harder was when there was too much content released at once—I would feel overwhelmed and not know where to start. Also, the discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, which made me less enthusiastic about participating. The gamified elements actually helped make some tasks feel lighter.
Q20: I think those students might be anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work, or maybe they're confused about what to do. They could also be dealing with personal issues that make it hard to focus enough to finish assignments. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes it just means someone is struggling to figure things out.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I performed well because I studied efficiently offline, not because I was constantly on the LMS. My low engagement score doesn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes and handwritten practice problems. Some of us just learn better away from computers, and that's not something the system can measure.
Q22: Course logs can show when you submitted things and how often you logged in, which indicates basic participation. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether you actually understood something or just clicked through it. They also miss offline studying, emotional state, and the real effort behind each login. Numbers don't tell the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious about what I'm learning and want to do well because the knowledge matters, not just the grade. For me, real engagement happens when I'm actively thinking and connecting ideas, even if I'm just sitting quietly with my notes.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally while still maintaining my grades. It wouldn't capture the sadness I carried or the stress of managing everything with limited resources. It also wouldn't show my offline dedication—the early mornings, the handwritten notes, the determination to succeed despite everything. My engagement was deeper than any log could record.
